title: Inflation Is Not Prices: The Printing Press vs. the Fever
body_html:
One of the essay’s central rhetorical moves is to reclaim the meaning of inflation. Inflation, in the authors’ telling, is not ‘prices going up.’ It is the deliberate expansion of the money supply. Rising prices are what often follow, but they are the fever, not the infection. That matters because whoever controls the definition controls the blame.

If inflation is defined only as a rise in prices, the state can always point elsewhere for the cause. It can blame merchants, supply chains, consumer psychology, foreigners, weather, pandemics, or greedy corporations. But once inflation is understood as monetary expansion, the line of accountability points back toward the issuer of money and the institutions that benefit from suppressing that fact.

This is more than semantics. It changes how people interpret policy. If the printer causes the distortion, then more printing is not neutral management but active intervention with distributional consequences. Savers lose purchasing power, asset holders often benefit first, and political systems gain room to finance themselves without transparent taxation.

The essay’s point is that fiat economics survives partly by mangling language. Rename the cause as the symptom, and the regime can present itself as doctor rather than arsonist.
